classification.title: AN ACT in aid of the Common Schools of this Commonwealth
classification.summary: This act addresses financial and administrative issues within the common school system for the school year ending June 30, 1874. It allows for shorter school terms in districts with fewer than fifty census children and authorizes the Superintendent of Public Instruction to make an early payment of 40% of the amount due to schools that have taught at least half their term by January 10, 1874. The act also aims to harmonize school tax payment timelines with teacher payment schedules.

CHAPTER 19 .
AN ACT appropriatingmoney totheFourthKentucky LunaticAsylum.
WHEREAS ,By an act of theGeneral Assembly ,entitled"An
act concerningthe variouscharitableinstitutionsin thisCom-
monwealth ," the buildingsformerlyerectedand designed as a
State House of Reform for Juvenile Delinquents , were con-
verted intoan asylum for the reception,care,and treatment
of that classof lunaticsafflictedwith chronic mania or epi-
lepsy, under the name of the " Fourth Kentucky Lunatic
Asylum ," and the Governor was directed" to cause all the
lunaticsin the State to be locatedand provided for in con-
formity with said act;" but no provision was made under
which he could draw money from the Treasury for that pur-
pose ; and whereas ,it appears from the Governor's message
that,in order to carry out the directionsof said act,itwas
indispensablynecessaryto make extensivechanges and im-
provements in and upon said buildings, as well as in pro-
viding water supplies, heating apparatus, furniture, & c., to
provide for which he borrowed from the Commissioners of
the Sinking Fund the sum of fiftythousand dollars,forwhich
he gave hisbond as Governor of the Stateof Kentucky ; and
whereas,it appears from the said message that the contracts
made by the Governor for these necessary changes and im-
provements amount to the sum of sixtythousand four hund-
red dollars, and that the work has so far progressed as to
furnishaccommodations formore than one hundred and forty
inmates, and is expected to be completed at an earlydate;
and whereas , it is manifest that unless said loan had been
made to the Governor by the Commissioners of the Sinking
Fund ,said asylum could not have been occupied or used as
such for nearly or quite two years ,and thus left the patients
now confined in that institutionunprovided for; therefore,Be itenactedby the GeneralAssemblyof the Commonwealth of
Kentucky :
§ 1. That the saidaction and proceedingsof the Governor
and the Commissioners of the Sinking Fund areapproved and
confirmedby thisGeneralAssembly,and thatthesaidsum of
sixtythousand four hundred dollarsis hereby appropriated
to pay forthe work alreadycompleted or under contract,and
the other necessaryexpendituresfor said asylum; and that
the Auditor of Public Accounts is directed to draw his war-
rant therefor in favor of the Governor of this Commonwealth ,
and that the same shallbe paid out of the publicTreasury :
Provided,however, That the aforesaid bond for fiftythousand
dollarsgiven by the Governor to the Commissioners of the
Sinking Fund shallbe deliveredto him as part of thisappro-
priation,and the Governor ishereby requiredto make report
to the General Assembly , showing how or for what said
money has been or shallbe expended .
§ 2. It isfurtherenactedthatthisact shalltake effectfrom
itspassage .
Approved December 22, 1873.

classification.title: AN ACT to regulate the sale of spirituous, vinous, or malt liquors in this Commonwealth .
classification.summary: This act outlines the process for regulating the sale of alcoholic beverages within Kentucky. It mandates that county judges, upon receiving a petition from at least twenty legal voters in a district, town, or city, must order an election to determine if liquor sales should be permitted. The county clerk is responsible for providing the sheriff with a certified copy of the judge's order, and the sheriff must publish this order in a local newspaper and post handbills in conspicuous places to notify the public of the upcoming vote.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of alcohol sales through local option elections. It does not contain any explicit or implicit racial components. The process described applies to all legal voters within a district, town, or city, regardless of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Regulate the Sale of Spirituous, Vinous, or Malt Liquors in Districts, Towns, or Cities
classification.summary: This act outlines the process for local option elections regarding the sale of alcoholic beverages. It details the requirements for petitions, election notices, the voting process, certification of results, and penalties for selling liquor after a majority vote against it. Exceptions are made for manufacturers, wholesale dealers, and druggists selling for medicinal purposes with a physician's prescription. The election can be held no more than every two years.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns local option elections for the sale of alcoholic beverages. It does not mention race or any racial classifications. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as an act passed by the General Assembly in 1874. The confidence is high because the content is clearly about liquor regulation and has no apparent racial component. The category is null because it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to provide for the registration of marriages, births, and deaths.
classification.summary: This act mandates that clergymen, physicians, surgeons, and midwives keep registries of marriages, births, and deaths they attend. These registries must include specific details about the individuals involved, such as names, ages, residences, places of birth, sex, and color (in the case of births and deaths). The registries or copies thereof are to be deposited annually with the county clerk and then transmitted to the Auditor of Public Accounts by assessors. The act also outlines fees for assessors.
classification.reasoning: The statute requires the recording of 'color' for births and deaths. While this statute itself does not enact discriminatory practices, the explicit requirement to record race in vital statistics is a common feature of systems that later facilitated or enforced Jim Crow policies. However, the statute's primary purpose is the collection of vital records, not the enforcement of segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law in itself, but it does contain a racial indicator. The confidence is high because the statute's intent is clearly about record-keeping, not discrimination. The racial indicator is explicit due to the mention of 'color'.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled 'An Act to provide for the registration of births, marriages, and deaths in this Commonwealth', approved March 17, 1871.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning the registration of births, marriages, and deaths. It details the duties of the Auditor in creating tabular statements and printing them, the responsibilities of county court clerks in preserving records, and the evidentiary value of these records in court. It also outlines penalties for failing to comply with the act and grants assessors the authority to administer oaths. The act specifies that assessors can record births, marriages, or deaths that occurred prior to the current list's embrace, with a fee for such entries.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the administrative process of recording vital statistics (births, marriages, and deaths) and does not contain any provisions that discriminate based on race. It focuses on the collection, preservation, and evidentiary use of these records. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the jurisdiction of justices of the peace in certain counties.
classification.summary: This act grants justices of the peace in a specified list of Kentucky counties concurrent jurisdiction with circuit and quarterly courts for actions involving the recovery of money or personal property up to one hundred dollars. It also outlines procedures for pleading, filing claims, and paying taxes on claims. The act further details appeal processes to quarterly and circuit courts based on the amount in controversy and repeals conflicting laws in the mentioned counties. It excludes the Carrsville district in Livingston county and takes effect in May 1874.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the jurisdiction and procedures for justices of the peace in civil cases involving small monetary amounts. It lists specific counties where these provisions apply and details the process for filing claims, paying taxes, and making appeals. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute appears to be purely procedural and administrative in nature, related to the functioning of the judicial system for small claims. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act amends Chapter 75 of the General Statutes concerning Master Commissioners, Receivers, and other Commissioners in Equity. It allows a judge to fill a vacancy in the office of commissioner or receiver during vacation by appointment, which must be entered of record at the next term. The appointed commissioner or receiver must take an oath and execute a bond, and will have the power to perform all duties of the predecessor, including executing unexecuted orders or judgments, provided the reasons for their acting are stated in their report.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details amendments to the process of appointing and qualifying Master Commissioners and Receivers in Kentucky courts. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to regulate the responsibility of innkeepers.
classification.summary: This act outlines the responsibilities of innkeepers regarding the safekeeping of guest valuables. It states that innkeepers are not liable for the loss of certain valuables (money, jewelry, etc.) if they provide an iron safe and conspicuously post notices about its availability and the act itself. However, innkeepers remain liable for losses caused by their own or their servants' theft or negligence. The act also specifies penalties for innkeepers who embezzle guest property.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the liability of innkeepers for the loss of guest property. It details requirements for innkeepers to provide safes and post notices to limit their liability. The text does not contain any explicit or implicit references to race, segregation, or differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'CHAPTER 199' and 'AN ACT to regulate...'. The confidence is high because the statute's content is clearly unrelated to racial discrimination.

Partners may, however, assist in prosecutions where the Commonwealth is the plaintiff. Violators face a fine of $100 to $500.

classification.title: An Act to provide for a Geological and Mineralogical Survey of the State
classification.summary: This act authorizes and requires the State Geologist to conduct a survey of the state's water powers, study its fishes, and examine other animals of economic or scientific importance. It also outlines the funding for the survey's publication, allows directors to access library resources and maps, and empowers them to publish results in journals. The act further authorizes the organization of topographical corps to develop mineral resources and construct geographical and geological maps. It also re-enacts provisions from a previous act concerning the geological and mineralogical survey.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the organization and funding of a geological and mineralogical survey of Kentucky. It focuses on natural resources, scientific study, and mapping. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly in 1874. The confidence is high because the content is clearly unrelated to racial discrimination.

classification.title: AN ACT concerning the State House of Reform, and permanently establishing the Central Kentucky Lunatic Asylum.
classification.summary: This act converts the State House of Reform for Juvenile Delinquents into the Central Kentucky Lunatic Asylum. It appropriates $100,000 for the construction of separate facilities for colored lunatics and additional accommodations for white individuals. The act mandates that colored lunatics be kept separate and apart from white lunatics.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mandates the separation of 'colored lunatics' from 'white lunatics' in the newly established Central Kentucky Lunatic Asylum. This clear segregation of individuals based on race in a public institution is a hallmark of Jim Crow legislation. The appropriation of funds specifically for separate accommodations for different racial groups further solidifies its classification as a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the chapter designation. The confidence is high due to the explicit racial segregation mentioned. No human review is needed as the statute's intent is clear.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act for the benefit of the officers and soldiers of the 31st regiment, enrolled militia," approved February 7, 1866.
classification.summary: This act repeals a portion of a previous act that authorized the Governor to pay officers and soldiers of the 31st regiment of the enrolled militia for service in Carter County in 1864. It then authorizes the Quarter-Master General to hear proof of and audit claims for services rendered by these officers and soldiers, with payment to be made from funds collected from the United States Government on war debt. The rates of pay are to be the same as allowed by the laws of the United States at the time the services were rendered.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the payment of claims for military service rendered by the 31st regiment of the enrolled militia in Carter County, Kentucky, during 1864. It amends a previous act and outlines the process for auditing and paying these claims, specifying that payments will be made from federal funds collected on war debt. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to establish a criminal court in certain counties
classification.summary: This act establishes a criminal court in several Kentucky counties, defining its jurisdiction, terms, and procedures. It grants the court the same jurisdiction as circuit courts in criminal and penal causes, concurrent jurisdiction in lunacy and idiocy inquests, and exclusive jurisdiction in allowing claims connected to its business. The act also outlines provisions for extending court terms, appointing special terms, making rules, handling appeals, and the duties and compensation of the Commonwealth's attorney. It further specifies that the selection, summoning, and payment of jurors, as well as the return of bail bonds, will be governed by laws applicable to circuit courts.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the establishment and operational procedures of a criminal court in several Kentucky counties. It outlines the court's jurisdiction, the schedule of its regular terms, its authority to extend terms or call special terms, and its procedural rules regarding appeals, contempt, and the Commonwealth's attorney. The statute also specifies that jury selection, summoning, and payment will follow existing laws for circuit courts. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute appears to be a procedural or organizational law for the court system and does not fall under the typical categories of Jim Crow legislation. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a high confidence, and the category is null.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Reorganize the Criminal Court System of Kentucky
classification.summary: This act establishes and reorganizes criminal courts in Kentucky, defining their jurisdiction, the selection of judges and jurors, and the transfer of duties from circuit courts. The act also outlines provisions for special judges and the interaction between criminal and circuit courts. It was approved on February 20, 1874, and was to remain in force for six years.

The act also details the specific methods and instruments to be used for testing the flammability of these oils.

It establishes procedures for testing the flash point of these oils and outlines the appointment and duties of inspectors. The act also outlines penalties for selling uninspected or condemned oils and for obstructing inspectors.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the law concerning the State Lunatic Asylums and persons of unsound mind.
classification.summary: This act amends the law regarding the admission and payment for patients in Kentucky's State Lunatic Asylums. It requires advance payment and security for board for most patients, with exceptions for paupers and those committed due to insanity after a crime acquittal. It also clarifies the definition of a pauper for married individuals and allows asylums to sue for unpaid board from patients or their responsible relatives if they acquire or possess sufficient estate. The commissioners can charge up to four dollars per week for paying patients.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the administration and financial aspects of state lunatic asylums. It details admission requirements, payment obligations for patients and their families, and the definition of a pauper in the context of mental health care. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the year '1874'.

The board is empowered to appoint officers and make necessary rules and regulations.

classification.title: An Act to amend and re-enact an act entitled "An act to provide for the government and management of the Kentucky penitentiary," approved March 10, 1874.
classification.summary: This act outlines the operational procedures for the board of commissioners and officers of the Kentucky penitentiary, including requirements for meetings, signing of proceedings, oaths of office, salaries of appointed officials (Medical Superintendent, Assistant Physician, Steward), and the execution of a bond by the treasurer for the safe-keeping and disbursement of funds. It specifies that claims against the institution must be approved by the board before payment and details the process for issuing warrants.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the administrative and financial regulations for the Kentucky penitentiary. It focuses on the governance structure, the duties and responsibilities of the board and its officers, and financial management. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 53 1874 .' and the content describing an act passed in that year.

classification.title: An Act to amend and reduce into one the several acts in relation to the Penitentiary
classification.summary: This section outlines the duties of the steward in procuring supplies, managing the farm and garden, and accounting for expenditures and products for an institution. It also details the age limits and duration of stay for children in the institution and provisions for their removal and transportation costs. Section 13 requires the secretary and treasurer to provide financial statements. Section 14 sets the age range for children received into the institution (6-18 years) and a maximum stay of 10 years, with provisions for removal by the board and state-funded transportation if parents/guardians are unable to pay.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the administrative and operational duties of a steward within an institution, including procurement of supplies, management of farm and garden, and financial reporting. It also specifies age restrictions and removal policies for children within the institution. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

The act also includes a section (Chapter 452) aimed at protecting citizens from incompetent physicians and surgeons.

classification.title: An Act to Regulate the Practice of Medicine
classification.summary: This act establishes requirements for practicing medicine in Kentucky, including graduation from a chartered medical school or a certificate of qualification from a board of examiners. It also outlines provisions for individuals with prior practice experience and details the appointment and duties of medical boards of examiners. Applicants must demonstrate good moral character and pay an examination fee.
classification.reasoning: The statute regulates the practice of medicine by requiring qualifications such as graduation from a medical school or a certificate from a board of examiners. It also mandates good moral character and sets examination fees. There is no mention of race, and the regulations apply to all individuals seeking to practice medicine. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the year '1874'.

classification.title: AN ACT to regulate the practice of medicine
classification.summary: This act establishes a board of examiners to grant certificates of qualification for the practice of medicine. It outlines the requirements for obtaining a certificate, the duration of its validity, and the fees to be paid by applicants. The act also specifies penalties for practicing medicine without a valid certificate, including fines and imprisonment. A provision exempts dentists from these regulations.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of medical practice and licensing. It sets up a system for examining and certifying medical practitioners. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The penalties and requirements apply to all individuals practicing medicine. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act details the duties of the Auditor of Public Accounts in preparing and distributing tax-related blanks and forms to various county officials. It outlines specific taxes and fees to be collected by sheriffs and other officers, including those for licenses related to taverns, coffee-houses, merchants selling liquor, playing cards, billiard tables, bowling alleys, menageries, circuses, theatrical performances, stud-horses, jacks, bulls, brokers, exchange dealers, and peddlers. classification.summary: This act outlines the reporting and accountability procedures for tax collectors and circuit court judges. It mandates that tax collectors report the number of persons who paid taxes and the aggregate amounts collected to the circuit court judge under oath. Judges are required to ensure these reports are made, attest to them, and forward them to the Auditor of Public Accounts. The Auditor is instructed not to issue monthly salaries to circuit judges until these reports are filed. The act also details the duties of officers in paying over collected moneys and settling their accounts after each circuit court term.

classification.title: An Act to provide for the education of the colored children of this Commonwealth
classification.summary: This act establishes a separate fund for the education of colored children in Kentucky. It outlines revenue sources, including federal land distribution and donations specifically for colored children. The act mandates separate accounting for the colored school fund, a separate census of colored children, and specifies that the distribution of funds and the census-taking process for colored children should mirror that of white children. It also details the responsibilities of clerks, judges, assessors, sheriffs, and the Auditor in managing and distributing these funds. The sheriff is allowed a percentage for collecting taxes for the colored school fund, and the county school commissioner receives compensation for visiting colored schools and for moneys disbursed.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'colored children' and establishes a separate system for their education, including separate funds, accounting, and census-taking. While it aims to provide education, the segregation inherent in establishing a separate fund and process for 'colored children' aligns with the discriminatory practices of the Jim Crow era. The language 'pro rata share to each colored pupil child shall not exceed...the apportionment made to each white pupil child' also indicates a potential for unequal treatment. Therefore, it is classified as a Jim Crow law in the education category.

classification.title: An Act to provide for the education of colored children.
classification.summary: This act establishes a system for the education of colored children in Kentucky. It mandates the division of counties into school districts for colored children, with specific pupil-age requirements per district. It outlines the appointment of colored school trustees, their duties in managing schools, employing teachers, and notifying parents. The act also details the process for obtaining school sites and erecting schoolhouses. Qualifications for teachers are specified, allowing for simpler examinations than for white schools. Donations for colored schools are permitted. Crucially, Section 16 explicitly prohibits colored children from attending white schools and vice versa. Teachers are required to teach a minimum of six hours daily and report attendance data.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mandates separate educational facilities for 'colored children' and prohibits them from attending schools for 'white children' (Section 16). This is a clear indicator of Jim Crow segregation in education. The entire act is structured around providing separate, and implicitly unequal, educational provisions based on race. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' because it is presented as 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from a specific year (1874) and refers to legislative actions ('GenAssemb 1874.pdf'). The confidence is high due to the explicit segregation clause. Human review is not needed as the Jim Crow nature is unambiguous.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend article three of chapter five of the General Statutes.
classification.summary: This act amends Article 3 of Chapter 5 of the General Statutes by assigning the duties of Auditor's and revenue agents to county attorneys. It re-enacts specific sections of Chapter 93 of the General Statutes, replacing the term 'agent' with 'county attorney'. The county attorney is prohibited from collecting or receipting money but is responsible for ensuring all revenue sources are listed and reported to the Auditor. They are also tasked with identifying corporations liable for taxes and will receive a 10% compensation on taxes collected from newly reported corporations. Additionally, county attorneys will receive 10% of all sums they cause to be paid into the Treasury. A separate act repeals Chapter 93 of the General Statutes, titled 'Revenue Agent'.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute, Chapter 524 of the 1874 Laws of Kentucky, along with the repealing Chapter 525, deals with the administrative duties of county attorneys regarding revenue collection and reporting. It reassigns responsibilities previously held by revenue agents to county attorneys. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The focus is purely on fiscal administration. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to authorize the voters of the city of Mayfield to vote upon the proposition as to whether spirituous, malt, or vinous liquors shall be sold within the corporate limits of said city, or within one mile thereof.
classification.summary: This act allows the voters of Mayfield, Kentucky, to decide through an election on December 22, 1873, whether to permit the sale of alcoholic beverages within the city or within one mile of its corporate limits. The election will have polls for and against the proposition, and the county court will be prohibited from issuing licenses if a majority votes against the sale.

classification.summary: This act outlines a process for the city of Mayfield, Graves County, to vote on whether to allow the sale of liquors. If the vote is against the sale, the county judge and city authorities are prohibited from granting licenses to vend liquors within the city or one mile thereof, except to bona fide druggists. Penalties are established for selling, giving, or furnishing liquors in violation of the vote, with enforcement mechanisms involving city warrants, arrests, fines, and potential jail time. The act also specifies the duties of the city marshal and allows for the issuance of warrants and enforcement by county sheriffs or constables.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns local option elections regarding the sale of liquor in Mayfield, Graves County. It details the process for voting, licensing, penalties, and enforcement. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against a specific racial group. The focus is on local control over alcohol sales. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the header '98 LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 1873 .'.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Re-enact Chapter 116 of the General Statutes, Title 'Intoxicating Liquors', So as to Regulate the Sale of Spirituous Liquors in the City of Louisville.
classification.summary: This act regulates the sale of spirituous liquors in Louisville, Kentucky, restricting sales to medical and sacramental purposes only. It requires licenses, oaths, and bonds for sellers, and mandates physician certificates for medical sales. Penalties are outlined for violations, including fines and forfeiture of licenses. Physicians are also penalized for prescribing liquors for non-medical or non-essential purposes.
classification.reasoning: The statute focuses on regulating the sale of spirituous liquors for specific purposes (medical and sacramental) and outlines licensing and penalty structures. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against a specific racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly in 1874. The confidence is high because the statute's content is clear and does not suggest any racial component.

classification.title: AN ACT to allow the Washington county court to subscribe stock in the Springfield, Maxville, and Harrodsburg Turnpike Road.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the Washington county court to subscribe to stock in the Springfield, Maxville, and Harrodsburg Turnpike Road company, up to fifteen hundred dollars per mile, to facilitate the completion of the road from Maxville to the Washington and Mercer county line. It also empowers a majority of the justices of the peace in Washington county to make this subscription and allows for a tax levy on county property to fund the stock purchase. The act takes effect upon its passage.

classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the Concord and Tollesboro Turnpike Road Company. It prohibits the use of 'rough lock' on wagons on the turnpike, requiring 'rub lock' instead, with a fine of five dollars for violations. It also grants the company the power to erect toll-gates and specifies penalties for sheriffs who fail to collect and remit taxes due to the company.

classification.title: AN ACT to authorize the Logan county court to borrow money to pay off the bonds issued by said county in aid of the Memphis Branch of the Louisville and Nashville Railroad Company.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the Logan county court to borrow up to $200,000 at an interest rate not exceeding 10% per annum to pay off bonds previously issued to aid the Memphis Branch of the Louisville and Nashville Railroad Company. The court is empowered to issue county bonds for this borrowed money and to levy an annual ad valorem tax on all taxable property in the county to pay the interest on these bonds. A sheriff will collect this tax. The county court can appoint an agent to sell the bonds and manage the proceeds, requiring a bond for faithful performance.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of Warren county.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the county court of Warren county to issue bonds, not exceeding eighty-five thousand dollars, to fund the county's floating debt and retire bonds issued in aid of the Louisville and Nashville Railroad Company that mature in April 1874. It also requires the treasurer and county judge to execute bonds for the safe-keeping of securities and moneys.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled 'An Act to provide for the payment of the bonded debt of Warren County,' approved March 10, 1873.
classification.summary: This act allows the Warren County Court to levy taxes to create a 'Miscellaneous Fund' for ordinary county expenses and a 'Warren County Sinking Fund' for bond interest and principal payments. It specifies a poll tax of three dollars on males over twenty-one for the Miscellaneous Fund and a poll tax of two dollars on males over twenty-one, along with a property tax, for the Sinking Fund. The sheriff is responsible for collecting these taxes.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the levying and collection of taxes for county funds, specifically for paying off bonded debt and ordinary expenses. It mentions a poll tax on 'each male over twenty-one years of age' but does not specify any racial distinctions or limitations. Therefore, it does not appear to be a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as an act passed by the General Assembly in 1874.

classification.title: AN ACT to incorporate the Christian Church at Warsaw

It vests title to existing property in designated trustees and their successors, and authorizes the trustees to sell and convey church property, including the 'Old Christian Church property' previously sold to the town trustees. The church is also empowered to pass by-laws for its secular and financial affairs.
classification.reasoning: The statute is an act to incorporate a specific church. It deals with the legal and financial affairs of that religious institution. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly create racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of the Agricultural and Mechanical Association of the Colored People of Bourbon County.
classification.summary: This act prohibits the sale of refreshments and spirituous or intoxicating drinks within a half mile of the grounds of the Agricultural and Mechanical Association of the Colored People of Bourbon County during its fair, unless authorized by local authorities. Violators are subject to penalties prescribed in a previous act.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'the Colored People of Bourbon County' in its title and in the context of an association. It then proceeds to regulate activities (selling refreshments and drinks) around the association's grounds during its fair. While it doesn't explicitly segregate, the very existence of an association for 'Colored People' and the regulation of activities around it implies a racial distinction and potentially a form of control or separation, which is characteristic of Jim Crow era legislation. The prohibition is tied to an association specifically for a racial group, making it implicitly about managing interactions and commerce related to that group. Therefore, it is classified as Jim Crow with an explicit racial indicator.

classification.summary: This act repeals specific sections of a prior act concerning the regulation of spirituous liquors in Monticello. It grants the town's chairman and board of trustees the power to regulate the sale of malt and spirituous liquors, setting a license fee range of $150 to $300 per annum. No license will be issued without first obtaining the state-required license. Selling without the required license incurs a fine of $50 to $100 per offense.

Tipton, died before completing his duties. The county court is empowered to appoint a successor who will have the same powers and duties as the deceased collector.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text is an amendment to an act concerning railroad tax collection in Montgomery County. It deals with the administrative process of appointing a successor to a deceased tax collector. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices that would classify it as a Jim Crow law. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a confidence of 1.0, and the category is null. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The racial indicator is 'none'.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of Gallatin county.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of the common schools of Trigg and Ballard counties.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the surplus school fund for Trigg and Ballard counties to be drawn upon the order of the county court. The fund can be distributed by the commissioner of common schools as prescribed by law, or loaned out at interest if the county court deems it more advantageous. The commissioner is required to report any loaned bonds for approval and to provide an annual statement to the county court regarding the fund's condition.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the management and distribution of surplus school funds for two specific counties in Kentucky. It outlines procedures for drawing funds, loaning them out, and reporting on their status. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to regulate the sale of spirituous or intoxicating beverages in Lewis county.
classification.summary: This act prohibits county courts and other authorities in Lewis County from granting licenses to sell spirituous liquors or intoxicating beverages in quantities less than two gallons. It also stipulates that any such liquor sold in quantities of two gallons or less must be consumed on the premises of the purchaser or under their control. Violators are subject to penalties for keeping a tippling-house and civil liability for damages.
classification.reasoning: The statute regulates the sale and consumption of alcoholic beverages in Lewis County. It sets a minimum quantity for sales (two gallons) and restricts where the beverages can be consumed. There is no mention of race or any provisions that would disproportionately affect a specific racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an 'ACT' passed by the General Assembly. Confidence is high because the statute's content is clear and does not suggest any racial discrimination.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act approved March the 28th, 1872, entitled "An act authorizing the Boone county court to appoint trustees for the Burlington Burying-ground, and prescribing their duties."
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning the Burlington Burying-ground. It grants the trustees additional powers to accept personal property or money by devise, gift, or donation for the benefit of the grounds. They can also raise funds by subscription for beautification and repairs, and can sue to recover any devised or subscribed money or property. The trustees are authorized to purchase, devise, or gift real estate not exceeding ten acres within half a mile of Burlington for an additional or separate burying ground. Furthermore, they can sue for damages caused by trespassing livestock and may be granted an attachment against such stock. The trustees may be required by the county court to execute a bond for the faithful performance of their duties.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the administration and expansion of a specific burying ground in Burlington, Kentucky. It details the powers and duties of the trustees, including property acquisition, fundraising, and legal recourse for damages. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT relating to opening and repairing roads in Boone county.
classification.summary: This act divides Boone county into ten civil districts and further into four road districts each. It levies an annual tax of two dollars on individuals required to work on public roads, to be collected by the sheriff and expended in their respective road districts for road improvement. The assessor is tasked with listing all male inhabitants required to work on roads, and the sheriff is responsible for collecting and reporting these taxes.

It outlines the division of the county into road districts, the levying of a road tax on individuals required to work on public roads, and the duties of the assessor and sheriff in collecting this tax. classification.title: AN ACT to prevent the destruction of fish in the ponds, lakes, and creeks in the counties of Hickman, Fulton, and Ballard, in this Commonwealth.
classification.summary: This act prohibits the use of nets, seines, or poisonous substances to catch fish in the specified counties. It allows fishing with hook and line and exempts landowners. Violators face fines, and each day a net or seine remains in the water is a separate offense. County judges and justices of the peace are tasked with enforcing the act.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of fishing practices in specific Kentucky counties. It outlines prohibitions on certain methods of fishing, defines exceptions, and sets penalties for violations. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act defines the boundaries of Murray common school district No. 35 in Calloway County, Kentucky. It also authorizes the trustees of Murray to subscribe stock to the Murray Male and Female Institute, up to $1,000 per year for ten years, and continues an existing act for the benefit of the institute in full force for ten years. The act specifies the geographical boundaries of the school district and allows for the continuation of previously attached residents and children.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Hopkinsville Plow Manufacturing Company
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Hopkinsville Plow Manufacturing Company, outlining its capital stock, organizational structure, election of officers, and business operations. It specifies the company's capital stock not to exceed $500,000, divided into shares of $100 each, and that these shares are to be treated as personal property. The act details the election of a president, board of directors, secretary, and treasurer annually by stockholders, with each share granting one vote. It also allows for voting by proxy and states that company books are open for inspection by officers and stockholders.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the incorporation and operational framework of the Hopkinsville Plow Manufacturing Company. It covers aspects such as capital, governance, and shareholder rights. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 1874 .' and the content being an act passed by the General Assembly.

classification.title: AN ACT amending the charter of the city of Paducah, making a husband responsible for taxes due on his wife's property where he collects her rents or uses her property.
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of Paducah, Kentucky. It makes husbands responsible for taxes on their wife's property if they collect rents or use the property. The husband's personal property can be seized to pay these taxes. This responsibility also extends to fathers, guardians, agents, trustees, committees, or receivers who use, enjoy, or collect rents from property belonging to a child, ward, principal, cestui que trust, or person of unsound mind.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the financial responsibility for property taxes in the city of Paducah, specifically when a husband controls or benefits from his wife's property, or when other fiduciaries manage property for others. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly in a specific year.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the charter of the city of Louisville, and to provide for the punishment of offenses committed within its corporate limits.
classification.summary: This act allows cities to confine individuals convicted of minor offenses (fines not exceeding $100 or imprisonment not exceeding 50 days) in the city jail instead of the county jail. It also mandates that such individuals work on city streets or public works to satisfy their fines and costs, at a rate of one day per dollar. If they refuse to work, they are to be kept in the city jail. The city council is empowered to appoint a jail keeper and prescribe duties and work details.
classification.reasoning: The statute details provisions for municipal incarceration and labor for minor offenses. It does not contain any explicit or implicit racial classifications or distinctions. The focus is on the administration of justice within city limits and the use of inmate labor for public works, which was a common practice in many jurisdictions regardless of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to provide for the establishment and government of a public school in the district of Pleasant Hill, in the county of Mercer.
classification.summary: This act establishes a public school in Pleasant Hill, Mercer County, Kentucky. It outlines the election and terms of trustees, their powers and responsibilities in managing the school, including hiring teachers, setting salaries, prescribing curriculum, and admitting students. It specifies that all white children over six years of age within the district have equal rights to admission and that no fees will be charged. It also prohibits the teaching of any religious tenets or class books that reflect upon any religious denomination.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'white children' in Section 11, indicating a racial classification for educational access. While it grants equal rights to admission for all white children within the district, the explicit mention of 'white' implies a potential exclusion or separate treatment of non-white children, which is characteristic of Jim Crow era legislation. However, the statute itself does not mandate segregation or differential treatment based on race for those it explicitly includes. It is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow because it doesn't enforce segregation or discrimination against a specific racial group, but rather defines the eligible population for this particular school. The confidence is high because the racial indicator is explicit. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' from the header '1874. LAWS OF KENTUCKY'.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Laws in Relation to Paupers and the Support of the Poor in Mason County
classification.summary: This act outlines the conditions under which individuals can gain legal settlement in Mason County for the purpose of receiving poor relief. It specifies that residence as a pauper or public charge does not grant legal settlement. It also allows county officials to remove individuals who intend to become a public charge and denies them legal settlement if they fail to leave. The county court is obligated to provide relief to those without legal settlement, potentially by directing them to the county infirmary.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the legal settlement and support of paupers within Mason County. It establishes criteria for gaining settlement and outlines the process for removing individuals who are likely to become a public charge. There is no mention of race or any differential treatment based on race within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the year '1874'.

The board is required to visit the institution monthly.

While it mandates the separation of males and females, this is for general reasons of order and effectiveness, not explicitly for racial segregation. There is no mention of race or any indication of discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It also addresses the compensation of directors and the legal validity of gifts and bequests to the institution. A specific section addresses how to handle paupers admitted to the infirmary who own property.

Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled 'An Act to provide for the support of paupers and the management of the poor of Mason county,' approved March 10, 1871.
classification.summary: This act details the management of property belonging to paupers admitted to the infirmary in Mason County. It outlines procedures for the directors to take possession of, sell, and apply the proceeds from a pauper's property towards their maintenance. It also specifies how any remaining balance should be handled upon the pauper's death or discharge. Additionally, it defines 'legal settlement' within the context of the act and clarifies the geographical scope of 'Mason county' for different institutions.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the financial management of paupers within a specific county's infirmary. It details how the property of paupers can be used for their care and what happens to any remaining assets. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The focus is purely on the administration of poor relief and property rights of individuals receiving such aid. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act concerning the poor of Mason County and the city of Maysville.
classification.summary: This act details the financial and administrative arrangements for the care of the poor in Mason County, specifically addressing the relationship between the county and the city of Maysville. It outlines how Maysville can continue to manage its own poor and retain its tithe tax, or how it can integrate with the county's poorhouse system by paying its share of costs and taxes. The act also clarifies the applicability of Chapter 86 of the General Statutes of Kentucky regarding poor and poor-houses to Mason County, superseding it where conflicts arise. The act requires ratification by the Mason County court of claims to become effective.
classification.reasoning: The statute discusses the administration of poor relief in Mason County and the city of Maysville. It outlines financial and administrative procedures for managing the poor, including the collection and use of taxes. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The focus is purely on the governance and funding of public welfare services. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the legislature in 1874. The confidence is high because the content is clearly about poor relief and has no racial component.

classification.title: AN ACT in relation to the town of Newtown , in Scott county .
classification.summary: This act prohibits the Scott County Court from granting licenses to sell spirituous, vinous, or malt liquors within two miles of the town of Newtown. Any such license granted would be null and void, and individuals selling liquors in violation of this act would be subject to penalties for keeping a tippling house.

The second act (Chapter 164) is titled 'AN ACT in relation to the town of Newtown, in Scott county.' This second act prohibits the granting of liquor licenses within a two-mile radius of Newtown. There is no mention of race or any discriminatory provisions based on race in either act. Therefore, neither act is classified as Jim Crow. classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Boyd County Agricultural and Mechanical Association
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Boyd County Agricultural and Mechanical Association, outlining its objects, capital stock, governance structure, and the powers of its directors and officers. The association's purpose is to promote agricultural, mechanical, mining, manufacturing, stock-raising, and general industrial interests in its vicinity, including holding exhibitions and offering premiums. The capital stock is set at five thousand dollars, with the possibility of increasing to twenty thousand dollars, divided into shares of twenty dollars each. The board of directors is responsible for the association's prudential, financial, and other concerns, with the power to adopt bylaws and elect officers. The text specifies the election of a president, treasurer, vice presidents, and secretaries, and the requirement of bonds for officers.

The trustees are empowered to act as conservators of the peace and prosecute violations of law, including breach of peace, misdemeanors, drunkenness, profane swearing, and selling intoxicating liquors contrary to law. The act explicitly prohibits the board of trustees from granting licenses for the sale of spirituous, vinous, or malt liquors within the town, and forbids merchants, grocers, tavern-keepers, druggists, or any other persons from selling or offering such liquors for sale.

It outlines the responsibilities of the city treasurer as custodian of the fund's assets, procedures for selling pledged bonds if notes are not paid, reporting requirements for the commissioners to the common council, and the treasurer's accounting duties. It also specifies how money is to be paid out of the fund via warrants and how bonds are to be canceled and registered. Provisions are made for filling vacancies in the commissioners and for allowances to the treasurer.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to reduce the charter of the city of Henderson into one act, and to amend the same," approved March 10, 1871, and to repeal certain sections thereof.
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the city of Henderson, primarily concerning the terms of office for city officials, the abolition of the city clerk position, and changes to the jurisdiction and operation of the Henderson city court. Specifically, it repeals provisions allowing the Henderson city court to impanel a grand jury or hold quarterly/semi-annual terms for the trial of pleas of the Commonwealth, and abolishes its jurisdiction for offenses requiring indictment. The court retains jurisdiction for offenses within the city limits, except those requiring indictment, and can summon juries for trials where the right exists and is not waived, with no compensation for jurors.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the administrative and judicial structure of the city of Henderson, specifically the Henderson city court. It details changes in the court's jurisdiction, the abolition of grand jury impanelment for certain cases, and the requirement for indictment for specific offenses. While it touches upon the judicial system, there is no explicit or implicit mention of race, segregation, or differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not classified as a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly in 1874. The confidence is high because the text clearly focuses on municipal governance and court procedures without any racial undertones.

classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the town of Earlington by adding additional territory to its boundaries. It also clarifies that the common council of Henderson cannot appropriate money for public schools from the general treasury, but only from funds specifically collected for that purpose. The act also addresses the management of gas works and the duties of the gas board and treasurer.
classification.reasoning: The provided text contains two distinct acts. The first part (sections 23-26) deals with the management of gas works and the funding of public schools in the city of Henderson. Section 25 specifically states that the common council cannot use general treasury funds for public school expenses, but must use funds collected for that purpose. The second part (Chapter 196) is an act to amend the charter of the town of Earlington, primarily concerning the expansion of its territorial boundaries. Neither of these acts explicitly or implicitly mentions race or racial segregation. Therefore, they are not Jim Crow laws.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the "Kentucky Conference of the Methodist Episcopal Church"
classification.summary: This act grants corporate powers to the Kentucky Conference of the Methodist Episcopal Church, including the authority to make contracts, sue and be sued, and hold and sell real estate. It also prohibits certain commercial activities, such as peddling and selling liquor, within specified distances of camp meetings, and restricts transportation on the Sabbath day. The act empowers the board of directors to regulate provisions and boarding tents within the enclosure and to appoint police officers to maintain order.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the incorporation of a religious organization and the regulation of activities around its camp meetings. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1874.

classification.title: AN ACT to organize and establish a system of public schools in Owingsville, Bath county.
classification.summary: This act incorporates a specific territory as a school district in Owingsville, Bath County, to be managed by a board of five trustees. Crucially, it specifies that trustees are to be elected annually by 'legally qualified white voters' and any 'white widow' entitled to vote. This explicit racial qualification for voting in school board elections is a hallmark of Jim Crow era segregation in education.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states that trustees are to be elected by 'legally qualified white voters' and 'white widow'. This is a clear indicator of racial discrimination in the electoral process for public schools, a common feature of Jim Crow laws. Therefore, the statute is classified as 'yes' for Jim Crow. The confidence is high due to the explicit mention of race in the voting qualifications. The category is 'education' as it pertains to the organization of public schools. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the chapter structure. The racial indicator is 'explicit' because the statute directly names 'white voters'.

classification.title: An Act to amend and re-enact an act entitled "An act to provide for the support of schools in the county of Bath."
classification.summary: This act allows the board of trustees of schools in a specific district of Bath County to levy an annual tax on the property of white people and a capitation tax on white male voters residing in the district. The tax is for the sole purpose of supporting the schools. The levy requires a vote of approval from the legal white voters and widows allowed to vote. The sheriff of Bath County is responsible for collecting these taxes.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions levying taxes on 'white people' and 'white male voters' for the support of schools. This segregation of taxation based on race, even for educational purposes, is a characteristic of Jim Crow legislation. The confidence is high because the racial distinction is explicit and directly tied to the funding and governance of schools. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1874.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of the city of Louisville
classification.summary: This act concerns the financial management of the city of Louisville, specifically addressing the funding of debts owed to laborers, artisans, officers, and other persons employed by the municipal government. It allows for the issuance of bonds to cover these outstanding debts due to insufficient revenues in the previous fiscal year. The act also mentions that the sinking fund commissioners believe the proposed provisions are sufficient to cover the principal and interest of these bonds.
classification.reasoning: The provided text contains two distinct sections. The first section (pages 249-288) details provisions for the Bath Seminary, including trustee powers, census returns, fund appropriation, and bond issuance for school purposes. The second section, labeled 'CHAPTER 206', is titled 'AN ACT for the benefit of the city of Louisville'. This act addresses the city's financial obligations to its employees and the issuance of bonds to cover these debts. Neither section contains any language or provisions that suggest racial discrimination or segregation, which are characteristic of Jim Crow laws. Therefore, this statute is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a confidence of 1.0, and the category is null. classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of St. Charles
classification.summary: This act details the qualifications for voting in the town of St. Charles, requiring voters to be bona fide male citizens who have resided in the town for sixty days and paid all their taxes and fines due to the town. It also outlines the election process for trustees, the corporate structure of the board of trustees, the election of a president, the appointment of a clerk, and the powers of the trustees to pass by-laws and fill vacancies. The act does not contain any provisions that explicitly or implicitly discriminate based on race.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the incorporation and governance of the town of St. Charles, including voter qualifications and the election of town trustees. The voter qualifications are based on citizenship, residency, and tax payment, with no mention of race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly in 1874.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Hopkinsville
classification.summary: This act details the powers and fees of a judge in Hopkinsville, including the authority to compel property owners to make improvements such as paving. It also outlines the marshal's power to sell real estate for unpaid taxes. The act specifies fees for various judicial actions and establishes quarterly court sessions for civil cases, with penal and criminal cases to be tried promptly. The board of trustees is empowered to designate pavement specifications and enforce compliance, with a lien on the property for costs incurred if owners fail to comply. The act also grants the trustees the power to create and change a corporate seal.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the municipal governance and financial administration of the town of Hopkinsville. It outlines the powers and fees of a local judge and the board of trustees, including the enforcement of property improvements and tax collection. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of the city of Frankfort.
classification.summary: This act grants the city of Frankfort the power to borrow money to extinguish existing indebtedness. It allows the city council to issue bonds or notes, with terms not exceeding four years and interest not exceeding eight percent per annum. classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act authorizing the Lewis county court to levy an additional tax to pay off the debt of said county, and to aid in building turnpike roads in said county.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act concerning Lewis County's finances. It clarifies that an additional tax can only be levied to pay off existing county debt until it's settled. After the debt is paid, the county court can continue to levy the tax to aid in building turnpike roads. The county judge is restricted from issuing bonds for new turnpike roads or bridges, with an exception for the Dry Run and Kinniconick Turnpike Road. The county court retains the power to assist in building turnpike roads up to $1,000 per mile, with payments contingent on mile completion. A turnpike commissioner will be appointed annually to inspect newly built roads before county stock is paid.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with county taxation, debt, and the construction of turnpike roads in Lewis County, Kentucky. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It abolishes the office of road commissioner and assigns their duties to justices of the peace within their respective magisterial districts. Overseers are required to report to the justices, with penalties for failure to do so.

It details the roles and responsibilities of local officials like justices of the peace and overseers, and the process of managing road taxes and reports. classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to provide for the working of public roads in the several counties of this Commonwealth."
classification.summary: This act details the process for appointing road overseers, their duties, terms of office, and fees. It also outlines the responsibilities of county attorneys regarding road establishment and settlement with justices. The act specifies that all male persons over sixteen and under fifty are subject to road work, and overseers have the power to straighten or alter roads with written consent from landowners. Fines collected are to be used for road work within the precinct where assessed.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the administration and maintenance of public roads. It outlines the roles and responsibilities of various local officials (justices, county attorneys, overseers) and the obligations of citizens regarding road work. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The age requirement for road work (over 16 and under 50) applies universally. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act repeals a previous amendment to the charter of Flemingsburg and establishes that all town officers, including the police judge and town marshal, shall be elected for a term of one year. The first election under this act is to be held on the first Monday in August 1874, and annually thereafter, at the court-house. Existing officers will hold their positions until their successors are elected and qualified under this act. The act takes effect on the first Monday in August next.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the administrative and electoral procedures of the town of Flemingsburg, specifically regarding the terms of office for town officials and the timing of elections. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to incorporate the town of California, in Campbell county
classification.summary: This act incorporates the town of California in Campbell County, Kentucky. It outlines the process for electing trustees, defining their powers and duties including laying off the town limits, levying taxes, and appointing a chairman. It also establishes the election of a police judge and town marshal, and specifies qualifications for trustees and voters. The act states that general laws relating to towns will apply unless inconsistent with this act.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the incorporation and governance of the town of California. It outlines election procedures, powers of trustees, and the roles of a police judge and town marshal. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act establishes a new justices' district, number eleven, in Harlan County by taking portions from existing districts one, five, and six. It outlines the boundary of the new district and mandates the county judge to establish a voting place and readjust district lines. The new district will elect two justices of the peace and one constable.

It details the geographical boundaries, the process for establishing voting places, and the election of local officials (justices of the peace and constables). classification.title: AN ACT to incorporate the Society of Friends, of Garrard county, and authorize them to issue bonds to purchase a burial ground.
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Society of Friends of Garrard County, allowing them to sue and be sued, contract, and use a common seal. The society's objectives include aiding needy members, supporting children of deceased members, and providing burial services. It authorizes the purchase of up to ten acres for a burial ground, excluding land within Lancaster's corporate limits. Membership is restricted to colored male persons between eighteen and seventy years old, and the society can establish rules for fees and dues.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states that membership in the Society of Friends is composed of 'colored male persons'. While the purpose of the society is charitable and not directly discriminatory in the typical Jim Crow sense (like segregation in public spaces), the explicit racial restriction on membership makes it relevant to a Jim Crow archive. However, it does not fit the common categories of Jim Crow laws (education, housing, public accommodations, etc.). Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, but the explicit racial indicator and the nature of the restriction warrant a high confidence in this classification. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' header and the chapter numbering.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Mount Sterling
classification.summary: This act grants the board of trustees of the town of Mount Sterling the exclusive right to license, tax, and regulate the sale of spirituous, vinous, or malt liquors, as well as various businesses like saloons, coffee-houses, billiard-saloons, bowling-alleys, auctioneers, peddlers, brokers, gift enterprises, and taverns. It also allows them to tax shows and exhibitions within the town or a half-mile radius. The act specifies minimum and maximum tax amounts for liquor vendors and requires bonds and payment of taxes to the town trustees, in addition to any state taxes. It also outlines quorum requirements for the board and procedures for by-law adoption.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of businesses, specifically the sale of alcoholic beverages and other commercial activities within the town of Mount Sterling. It grants local authorities the power to license and tax these businesses. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The focus is purely on local economic and regulatory powers. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act authorizes the trustees of Mayfield school district, No. 43, in Graves county, to submit to the qualified white voters of the district a question on whether to impose an annual district tax on taxable property owned by white persons and a poll tax to fund the erection or purchase and furnishing of a school-house for white children within the school age. classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to transfer the equity causes from the Hickman circuit court to Hickman court of common pleas," approved March 11th , 1873
classification.summary: This act modifies a previous law concerning the transfer of equity causes between the Hickman circuit court and the Hickman court of common pleas. It specifies that only equity causes not yet partly acted upon by the circuit court judge should be transferred. It also clarifies that the circuit judge retains jurisdiction over causes they have already partly tried, but has no original jurisdiction in new equity causes while the common pleas court is active. New suits should be filed in the common pleas court.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the procedural transfer of legal cases between two courts in Hickman county. It outlines which cases should be transferred and which court retains jurisdiction. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to incorporate the Covington, Flemingsburg, and Pound Gap Railway Company," approved March 5th, 1873.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act incorporating the Covington, Flemingsburg, and Pound Gap Railway Company. It specifies that directors shall be elected annually on the first Tuesday in July, and absent stockholders can vote by proxy. It also outlines procedures for filling vacancies in the board of directors and clarifies that stockholders are only liable for the amount of stock they own. Additionally, it allows county courts, with the concurrence of a majority of justices and the county judge, to order an election for the county to subscribe vacant and unappropriated lands as stock in the company, provided a majority of qualified voters approve.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the incorporation and operation of a railway company, including provisions for director elections, stock liability, and land subscriptions by county courts. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of L. D. Padgett, of Pulaski county.
classification.summary: This act directs the Auditor of Public Accounts to draw a warrant on the Treasurer in favor of L. D. Padgett for services rendered in conveying a lunatic, identified as Henry Owens, of color, to the lunatic asylum at Anchorage, Kentucky. The asylum superintendent refused to admit the lunatic, requiring Padgett to return him to jail. The act aims to compensate Padgett for this service.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'Henry Owens, of color,' indicating a racial element. However, the core of the act is about compensating an individual for services related to transporting a person deemed a lunatic, and the refusal of the asylum to admit the individual. While race is mentioned, the statute's primary purpose is not to enforce segregation or racial discrimination in the manner typical of Jim Crow laws. It is more of a private relief bill. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a high confidence, and the racial indicator is 'explicit' due to the mention of 'of color'.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to incorporate the Cincinnati, Covington, and Cumberland Gap Railroad Company."
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act to incorporate the Cincinnati, Covington, and Cumberland Gap Railroad Company. It authorizes the company to begin construction of a portion of their road between Paris and Mount Sterling upon securing a $200,000 subscription. It also allows the company to adopt its own gauge, change its name and style, and extend its road from Paris to a point on the Ohio River opposite Vevay, or another point where the Chicago and South Atlantic Railroad strikes the Ohio River, but not further west than a point opposite Madison, Indiana. The act takes effect upon passage.

classification.summary: This act establishes a building committee for the town of Versailles, Kentucky. The committee is responsible for examining proposed building projects, estimating fire and casualty risks to adjacent properties, and granting or refusing permits for construction, additions, or removals within the town limits. It also outlines the committee's term of office, oath requirements, reporting duties, and powers to order the removal or demolition of buildings deemed to pose an unusual risk or to be detrimental to sanitary welfare. Penalties for non-compliance are also specified.

It establishes a building committee with powers to grant or refuse permits, assess risks, and order the removal of buildings. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The penalties and regulations apply universally to all persons within the town's corporate limits. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.
