classification.title: AN ACT for the relief of the sheriff of Hancock county.
classification.summary: This act releases the sheriff of Hancock county, G.W. Taylor, from certain financial penalties and damages related to the late payment of revenue for the years 1871 and 1872. It directs the Auditor to draw a warrant for a specific amount paid into the Treasury and remits damages adjudged against the sheriff in Franklin circuit court. A condition for relief on the 1872 revenue is that the sheriff must prove the five percent penalty was not collected from taxpayers.
classification.reasoning: The statute is an act for the relief of a specific sheriff regarding financial penalties for late revenue payments. It does not contain any language, explicit or implicit, that suggests racial discrimination or segregation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The confidence is high because the subject matter is purely financial and administrative. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly in a specific year.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of Allen, Harbeson & Co., of Augusta, in Bracken county.
classification.summary: This act grants special privileges to the banking firm of Allen, Harbeson & Co. in Augusta, Kentucky. It declares that promissory notes made negotiable and payable at their banking house, and inland bills discounted by them, are to be treated as foreign bills of exchange, allowing for joint and several remedies against drawers and endorsers. Additionally, it establishes a specific tax rate for the firm's money capital, to be paid annually to the State Treasury, in lieu of other taxes or bonuses on that capital. The General Assembly reserves the right to investigate the capital used and to alter or repeal the act.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the banking operations and taxation of a specific private firm, Allen, Harbeson & Co. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Reduce into One the Several Acts in Relation to the Sale of Spirituous, Vinous and Malt Liquors in Fulton County
classification.summary: This act regulates the sale of alcoholic beverages in Fulton County, Kentucky. It prohibits the sale of such liquors by most individuals and establishments, with exceptions for medical use upon a physician's written certificate. Druggists can be licensed to sell for medicinal purposes after posting bond. Physicians are prohibited from granting certificates for beverages and must take an oath to conform to the act's provisions. Penalties are established for violations.
classification.reasoning: The statute focuses on regulating the sale of alcoholic beverages in Fulton County, Kentucky. It establishes restrictions and penalties related to the sale and distribution of liquors, with specific provisions for medical use. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against a specific racial group. Therefore, it is not classified as a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the source 'GenAssemb 1874.pdf' and the context of legislative acts from a specific year.

classification.title: An Act to repeal laws allowing licenses to sell spirituous, vinous, or malt liquors in Fulton County and to submit prohibition to a vote.
classification.summary: This act repeals all existing laws that grant authority to county courts, judges, clerks, mayors, councils, or boards of trustees in Fulton County to issue licenses for the sale of spirituous, vinous, or malt liquors. It mandates that the question of prohibition for such sales be submitted to a vote of the legal and qualified voters of Fulton County. The act outlines the process for holding this election, including poll book preparation, notice requirements, appointment of election officers, and the certification of results. It also allows for the question to be resubmitted after one year if the initial vote is against prohibition.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of alcohol sales through a local option election in Fulton County, Kentucky. It does not contain any explicit or implicit racial components. The focus is solely on the legal framework for granting liquor licenses and the process for voters to decide on prohibition. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as an act from the Laws of Kentucky in 1874.

classification.summary: This act orders the county court of Meade county to hold an election on the question of prohibiting the sale of spirituous, vinous, or malt liquors in Garnettsville. The election is to be held on the first Saturday in May, 1874, within the Garnettsville precinct. The county court is to appoint the place of voting, judges, and a clerk, and the sheriff is to appoint a deputy to attend the voting place.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY
classification.summary: This section of the Laws of Kentucky from 1874 outlines the powers of a city board. It grants the board authority to prescribe duties, oaths, and bonds for city officers; appoint a board of health with powers to prevent or abate diseases; provide a pest-house; establish and control city prisons, poor-houses, work-houses, and police stations, ensuring distinct apartments for the infirm, indigent, vagrants, and those confined for crimes. The board can also prohibit animals from running at large, suppress vice (tippling-houses, gambling-houses, bawdy-houses, lotteries, policy offices), riots, disturbances, and indecent conduct. Furthermore, it can abate nuisances on private property, prohibit the manufacture and regulate the sale/storage of explosives, and provide and control a fire department.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the administrative and regulatory powers of a city board. It covers public health, law enforcement, social welfare, and safety. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The powers granted, such as providing distinct apartments in institutions, are framed in terms of infirmity, indigence, vagrancy, and criminal confinement, not race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend the charter of the city of Louisville, and to provide for the assessment and collection of taxes therein.
classification.summary: This act establishes a Board of Equalization in the city of Louisville, composed of the mayor and three citizens elected by the board of councilmen. The board's duties include examining tax lists, correcting errors, and equalizing property valuations. It outlines procedures for notifying property owners of proposed increases in assessed value and allows for appeals. The board also has the power to compel the attendance of witnesses and keep records of its proceedings. The act details the process for levying and collecting taxes for the year.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the process for establishing and operating a Board of Equalization for tax assessment and collection in Louisville. It details the composition of the board, its duties in examining and correcting tax lists, and procedures for property owners to appeal valuations. There is no mention of race, and the provisions appear to apply universally to all property owners within the city. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

This segregation of educational facilities based on race is a hallmark of Jim Crow laws.

classification.title: An Act to amend the charter of the city of Newport, relating to its board of education.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the powers and responsibilities of the board of education for the city of Newport. It details provisions for the management of schools, property, and funds, the election and qualifications of board members and school personnel, curriculum, pupil admissions, and financial reporting. It explicitly states that all children are entitled to equal rights of admission and benefits, and that no religious instruction or classbooks reflecting negatively on any religious denomination should be used. It also prohibits schools from interfering with the religious faith or creed of parents or pupils.

The language used suggests an intent for equal access to education for all children within the specified district. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This statute, from the Laws of Kentucky in 1874, outlines the powers and responsibilities of a city's board of school trustees. It details their ability to levy taxes for school purposes, manage school funds, and hire employees. Crucially, it explicitly grants the board the power to "provide and set apart a portion of said school fund for the maintenance and support of separate schools, wherein all the colored children residing in said city" shall be admitted. This provision mandates racial segregation in the city's schools.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Reenact Chapter 111 of the General Statutes, Relating to the Improvement of Streets and Alleys in Cities of the First, Second, and Third Classes
classification.summary: This statute outlines the process for cities of the first, second, and third classes to improve streets and alleys. It details how property owners abutting the improvements will be assessed for the costs, including the establishment of a special tax, lien enforcement, and limitations on the total cost relative to property value. It also establishes a board to oversee the work and handle complaints.
classification.reasoning: The statute discusses municipal improvements, specifically the financing and assessment of costs for street and alley improvements. It details the process of levying special taxes on abutting property owners and the establishment of liens. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The language and subject matter are purely administrative and financial concerning urban development. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Sidewalks and Street Railroads
classification.summary: This statute outlines the responsibilities and regulations for the construction, reconstruction, and repair of sidewalks, placing the cost on property owners. It also details the process for granting permits for street railroads, including public notice, proposals for passenger fares, and obtaining consent from property owners. The city engineer is given control over the construction of street railroads.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to municipal infrastructure, specifically sidewalks and street railroads. It details the financial responsibilities of property owners for sidewalk construction and repair, and outlines the process for approving and constructing street railroads. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend the charter of the city of Newport.
classification.summary: This act grants the city of Newport the authority to construct, maintain, and operate waterworks, including the power to condemn land for this purpose. It also ratifies and confirms bonds previously issued for the waterworks and establishes a tax levy and sinking fund for their operation and redemption. The act further defines penalties for injury to waterworks fixtures or the contamination of water supplied by them, classifying such offenses as misdemeanors with potential fines or imprisonment.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the municipal infrastructure of Newport, specifically the establishment and regulation of waterworks. It details the city's powers regarding land acquisition, financial instruments (bonds), taxation, and penalties for vandalism or contamination of the water supply. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend the charter of the Newport Water-Works
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the Newport Water-Works, making it unlawful to erect slaughter-houses or other offensive establishments that would pollute the Ohio River or its tributaries within two miles of the pump-house. It also establishes a board of trustees to manage the water-works, outlining their appointment, terms, powers, and duties. The act specifies that the board of councilmen retains authority over tax levies and sinking funds.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the management and protection of water-works in Newport, Kentucky. It addresses public health by prohibiting pollution of water sources. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Re-enact Chapter 33 of the General Statutes, Title 'Cities of the Second Class', and to Amend and Re-enact Chapter 34 of the General Statutes, Title 'Cities of the Third Class'
classification.summary: This statute details the powers and duties of city officials in cities of the second and third class in Kentucky, including the establishment and jurisdiction of a city court. It outlines the court's powers to pass ordinances, affix penalties for violations, and handle misdemeanors and breaches of peace. The text focuses on municipal governance and judicial administration.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text describes the judicial powers and administrative functions of a city court in Newport, Kentucky. It outlines the court's jurisdiction over misdemeanors, breaches of peace, and violations of city ordinances, as well as the penalties that can be imposed. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The statute pertains to general municipal law and judicial administration. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Re-enact Chapter 28 of the General Statutes, Title 'Cities of the First, Second, and Third Class,' so as to Apply to Cities of the Fourth Class, and to Confer Additional Powers Upon the City Courts of Cities of the Fourth Class.
classification.summary: This statute details the jurisdiction and operational procedures of a city court in a fourth-class city. It outlines powers related to civil and criminal matters, including handling claims up to one hundred dollars, cases of idiocy, lunacy, vagrancy, binding out orphan children, and condemning land for public purposes. It also specifies daily court sessions, precedence for city ordinance violations, and jurisdiction over nuisances, riots, disorderly conduct, unlawful assemblies, and breaches of the peace within the city and one mile of its limits. The court has concurrent jurisdiction with justices of the peace in these matters.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the powers and jurisdiction of a city court. It covers a range of civil and criminal matters, including the handling of nuisances and public order offenses. However, there is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. The statute appears to be a general grant of judicial power to a municipal court. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Compensation and Physician Duties
classification.summary: This statute outlines the duties and compensation for a city assessor and a city physician. The assessor is responsible for listing property for taxation, with exceptions for low-value property and property subject to license or specific taxes. The city physician is elected, must meet specific qualifications, and is tasked with providing medical care to inmates of city institutions (jail, work-house, station-house) and to indigent persons and their children within the city.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details administrative and public service roles within a city government, specifically concerning taxation and public health. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The duties described for the city physician, such as attending to the 'indigent, poor, and unfortunate' and providing services to those unable to afford medical care, are general in nature and do not suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This statute outlines the duties and responsibilities of a city physician and a market master in Kentucky. The city physician is tasked with promoting public health, preventing disease, and vaccinating poor children free of charge. The market master is responsible for supervising market areas, maintaining order, enforcing regulations, and ensuring cleanliness. Both positions are elected or appointed and compensated as determined by the board of councilmen.

It focuses on public health, sanitation, and the regulation of market spaces. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This statute outlines the election, qualifications, oath, bond, powers, and duties of a city wharf-master. It details their responsibilities in supervising wharves and landing places, enforcing regulations, managing the mooring of watercraft, collecting wharfage fees, and maintaining financial records. The wharf-master has the power to levy and distrain for unpaid wharfage.

It focuses on the regulation of wharves, the collection of fees, and the management of watercraft. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It outlines their responsibilities for the jail's management, the safe-keeping of inmates, record-keeping, maintaining order, and ensuring the separation of males and females. The statute also specifies the election process, qualifications, oath, bond, and compensation for the jailer.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text describes the administrative and operational duties of a city jailer. It focuses on the management of the jail, the care and security of individuals committed to its charge, and the enforcement of jail rules. While it mentions the separation of males and females, and making distinctions based on age and crime, there is no explicit or implicit mention of race. Therefore, this statute does not appear to be a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' due to the heading 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the year '1874'.

classification.title: AN ACT to authorize the city of Newport to increase her bonded indebtedness in aid of water-works.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the city of Newport to issue bonds up to one hundred thousand dollars to pay accruing interest on its water-works bonds. The bonds will be payable in twenty years with seven and three-tenths percent interest, payable semi-annually, and will be secured by a lien on the water-works and the city's taxable property. The city is restricted from issuing more than one-third of the total amount of bonds in any one year.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the financial authorization for the city of Newport to issue bonds for its water-works. It does not contain any language related to race, segregation, or discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly of Kentucky in 1874.

classification.title: An Act to Establish a Police Court in the Town of North Middleton
classification.summary: This act establishes a police court in the town of North Middleton, Kentucky, defining its jurisdiction and powers. It outlines the election process for the judge and marshal, abolishes the civil and criminal jurisdiction of the chairman of the board of trustees, details the powers and qualifications of the marshal, and requires state licenses for business operations. Penalties for unpaid fines include imprisonment or street labor.
classification.reasoning: The statute establishes a local police court and outlines its administrative and judicial functions. It does not contain any explicit or implicit racial classifications or restrictions. The penalties for unpaid fines, such as imprisonment or street labor, are applied generally and not based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the charter of Lebanon , in Marion county.
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of Lebanon, Kentucky, to grant the police court jurisdiction over vagrant persons and prostitutes. It allows the court to fine offenders between five and twenty-five dollars. If a fined person cannot pay or replevy the fine, they are to work on the streets during the day and be held in the county jail at night until the fine is worked out.
classification.reasoning: The statute addresses vagrancy and prostitution in Lebanon, Kentucky. It outlines penalties and work-out provisions for those convicted. There is no mention of race, and the provisions apply to any person fitting the description of vagrant or prostitute, regardless of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. Confidence is high because the statute's text is clear and does not contain any racial indicators or discriminatory provisions.

classification.title: An Act to Amend an Act Entitled "An Act to Provide for the Election of Directors of the Cynthiana and Vanceburg Turnpike Road Company," Approved March 10, 1870.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act concerning the election of directors for the Cynthiana and Vanceburg Turnpike Road Company. It details the property qualifications for voting, allowing one vote per $100 worth of property, with provisions for affidavits if property is not taxable under the act. It also outlines proxy voting rules and the duties of petitioners and commissioners in preparing assessment lists and conducting elections. The act specifies the notice period for elections and the process for returning election results and declaring elected directors.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the election of directors for a turnpike road company and outlines voting qualifications based on property ownership. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. The language and subject matter are purely administrative and economic, related to corporate governance and voting procedures. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to provide for the construction of turnpike roads in this Commonwealth," approved March 14, 1872, and to provide for the taxation of property for the construction of said roads.
classification.summary: This act details the process for assessing property owners within a defined survey area for the construction of a turnpike road. It outlines how benefits to landowners will be estimated and categorized, and how a tax will be levied based on these benefits to fund the road construction. The tax is limited to a maximum of two percent annually and for a period of five years. It also includes provisions for situations where property might be assessed for multiple roads.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text describes the process for levying taxes to fund the construction of turnpike roads. It details how property owners within a survey area are assessed based on the benefits they receive from the road. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1874, indicating it's an act passed during a legislative session.

It outlines procedures for stock sales and transfers, authorizes trustees to receive subscriptions and donations for capital stock not exceeding $25,000, and specifies how these funds can be used for purchasing grounds, erecting buildings, and acquiring necessary furniture and apparatus. It also grants trustees the power to collect subscriptions by law, acquire property, issue stock certificates, hold regular meetings, visit the academy, and borrow money to discharge indebtedness by mortgaging corporate assets. The trustees are also tasked with ascertaining profits at the close of each academic year.

classification.summary: This act amends the road laws for Boyd and Carter counties, specifying that individuals liable for road work shall not be required to work more than four days per year. It also provides an option to pay seventy-five cents for each day of notified work instead of performing the labor, with payment due within five days of notice.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to reduce into one all acts in relation to Barbourville."
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act concerning the town of Barbourville. It grants trustees the power to set license fees for coffee houses and liquor sales between $100 and $500. It also mandates the revocation of licenses for establishments where gambling occurs, or where minors are sold or given liquor. Liquor dealers are held responsible for damages resulting from such sales. Furthermore, it states that individuals who are currently vendors of ardent spirits are ineligible to be trustees, and if a trustee becomes a vendor after election, their office is vacated. The act also adjusts the election dates for town officers.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of liquor sales, licensing, and town governance in Barbourville. While it addresses issues like selling liquor to minors and gambling, there is no explicit or implicit mention of race. Therefore, it does not appear to be a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the text clearly outlines its scope and purpose without any racial implications.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of John P. Barrett, late sheriff of Ohio county.
classification.summary: This act grants John P. Barrett, the former sheriff of Ohio County, an additional two years from April 1, 1874, to collect any uncollected fee bills and taxes that he has already accounted for and paid. It also authorizes him or his former deputies to use the same powers as current sheriffs to distrain, levy, and sell property for the collection of these outstanding debts. However, Barrett and his sureties will be held responsible for any illegal seizures or proceedings under this act, and Barrett will be subject to existing penalties for issuing or collecting illegal fee bills.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the extension of time for a former sheriff to collect outstanding debts and taxes. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that would disproportionately affect any racial group. The act is administrative in nature, dealing with the collection of fees and taxes. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to incorporate Petersburg Cemetery Company
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Petersburg Cemetery Company, granting it the power to purchase, hold, and sell land for burial purposes. It outlines the management of the corporation by a board of trustees and specifies that the cemetery grounds are to be used exclusively for burial and horticultural purposes, free from public improvements and taxes.
classification.reasoning: The statute incorporates a cemetery company and outlines its powers and operations. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Petersburg Cemetery
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the Petersburg Cemetery, specifying the election and duties of its board of trustees. It details how trustees are elected, how vacancies are filled, and the qualifications for voting for trustees, which are tied to lot ownership in the cemetery. The act also outlines the oath trustees must take.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the governance and administration of a cemetery, specifically the Petersburg Cemetery. It outlines the process for electing trustees, filling vacancies, and the voting rights of lot owners. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The language used is purely administrative and procedural regarding the cemetery's management. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend the charter of the Petersburg Cemetery Company
classification.summary: This act details the powers and duties of the board of trustees of the Petersburg Cemetery Company, including their authority to remove improper monuments and fixtures. It also outlines penalties for violating cemetery by-laws, such as unauthorized entry, defacing property, or disturbing graves. Actions for violations are to be brought before magistrates or courts in Boone County, with fines benefiting the company. The sexton is empowered to arrest offenders.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute pertains to the governance and regulation of a specific cemetery, the Petersburg Cemetery Company. It outlines rules for conduct within the cemetery grounds, the powers of its trustees and sexton, and penalties for violations. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The statute appears to be a general regulation for cemetery management and public conduct within its premises. Therefore, it is not classified as a Jim Crow law.

CHAPTER 334 .
AN ACT to provide for the payment of expenses incurred by the Ohio
county court in taking care of a pauper lunatic.
WHEREAS , Thomas E. Brown , a pauper ,was ,by a legal
inquest,found to be a lunatic,in Ohio county, and was
ordered by the county judge of said Ohio county court to
the Western Lunatic Asylum at Hopkinsville, but was-
failed to be admitted because said asylum was full; and
on account of his condition, and being no other place
suitable,he was ordered to be placed in the poor-house of
said county , and was so placed there on the 13th day of
May , 1872, and has been an inmate of said poor -house
ever since, and to the present date ; and further,said lu-
natic has been provided by said county court with suitable
diet, lodgings, and medical attentionup to and including
this date, and is stillan inmate of said institution; now ,
therefore ,
Be itenactedby theGeneral Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky :
§1 . That the county judge of Ohio countyis hereby
authorized to certifyto the Auditor of Kentucky the ex-
penses incurred in taking care of said Thomas E. Brown
from the 13th day of May , 1872,the time of his commit-
ment to the poor -house , until the 1st day of October ,
1873 ; and thereupon the Auditor shall draw his warrant
upon the Treasurer of the State of Kentucky in favor of
the county court of Ohio county for the amount that may
be due said county aforesaid: Provided,however, It shall
not exceed two hundred and seventy dollars.
§ 2. This act shall take effect from its passage .
Approved February 19 , 1874 .

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the charter of the town of Mt. Carmel, in Fleming county.
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the town of Mt. Carmel, Fleming County. It grants the town trustees the power to compel property owners to grade and pave sidewalks in front of their lots, with provisions for notice and recovery of expenses if owners fail to comply. The trustees are also authorized to pass ordinances to prevent hogs and cattle from running at large in the town's streets and alleys.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns municipal improvements (sidewalk paving) and animal control within the town of Mt. Carmel. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act for the benefit of the Stoner Mouth Presbyterian Church, Bourbon county.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act concerning the Stoner Mouth Presbyterian Church in Bourbon County. It addresses the failure of previously appointed trustees to discharge their duties due to a large, unprotected graveyard on the church property. The amendment vests property in new trustees (Willis Collins, James H. Ardery, and John I. Moore) specifically for the graveyard. These trustees are authorized to sell vacant land and the ruins of the old church building to fund the construction of a substantial stone wall around the graveyard. Any surplus funds are to be accounted for to the Bourbon County Court and invested to maintain the graveyard. Vacancies in the board of trustees are to be filled by the Bourbon County Court.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the administration of property for a specific church and its graveyard. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the charter of the city of Dayton
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the city of Dayton by allowing the city council to increase bonded indebtedness up to $100,000. It also requires contracts over $500 (except for street improvements) to be submitted to a vote of qualified voters. The act also details changes to councilman elections and the election of a single city assessor. Finally, it mandates the election of three commissioners to redistrict the city into four wards.
classification.reasoning: The statute is an amendment to the city charter of Dayton, Kentucky, from 1874. It deals with financial limits, contract approvals, election procedures for city officials, and city redistricting. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act for the benefit of the Union county court," approved December 20th, 1871, and to enable the county court of Union county to provide means for building a jail.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the presiding judge of the Union county court to issue bonds up to $25,000 to fund the construction, heating, and furnishing of a new jail. The judge will determine bond denominations, sale times and places. Bonds will mature in 15 years with 10% annual interest, redeemable after 12 months. Interest coupons will be attached. The levy court is empowered to levy a poll tax of $0.50 and an ad valorem tax of up to $0.10 per $100 of taxable property to pay off the bonds and interest.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the financing and construction of a jail in Union County, Kentucky. It details the process of issuing bonds, setting interest rates, and levying taxes to fund the project. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act incorporates the Owensboro and Glasgow Railroad Company, granting it the authority to construct and operate a railway from the Ohio River near Owensboro to the Tennessee State line, passing through Glasgow. It also grants the company powers to mine for petroleum, coal, ores, and minerals, and to prepare and transport these resources to market. The act details the company's capital stock, the rights to purchase property, and the powers of its directors.

There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. classification.summary: This statute, from the Laws of Kentucky in 1874, outlines the powers of a company regarding its capital stock, land acquisition, and construction of infrastructure. It details how the company can manage its stock, including subscription terms and forfeiture. It also grants the company the right to enter, survey, and use land for its road, depots, and other necessary structures. The statute specifies procedures for acquiring materials and land, including agreements with owners and, if agreements fail, a process involving county courts and appointed commissioners to determine compensation. The commissioners' report is to describe the land, materials, and assessed damages.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the operational and land acquisition powers of a company, likely a railroad, in 1874 Kentucky. It covers aspects of corporate finance, property rights, and eminent domain procedures. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The language used is general and applies to all property owners and citizens. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Louisville, Harrodsburg and Richmond Railroad Company
classification.summary: This act grants a railroad company the authority to connect its roads, consolidate with other companies, and assign or transfer franchises and stock. It also allows the company to sell or lease its rights and properties. The company can issue and sell bonds, not exceeding twenty-five thousand dollars per linear mile, with interest not exceeding ten percent. To secure these bonds, the company can execute a mortgage or deed of trust. The act also outlines procedures for foreclosure proceedings, ensuring they are for the benefit of all bondholders and require public notice.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the incorporation of a railroad company, outlining its powers related to road construction, consolidation, financing (issuing bonds and mortgages), and foreclosure procedures. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly in 1874. The confidence is high because the content is clearly about corporate and financial matters of a railroad, with no racial implications.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to incorporate Princeton College," approved February 20th, 1860,and to confercertainpowers on the board of trustees of the town of Princeton.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the board of trustees of Princeton to acquire stock in Princeton College. It also empowers them to issue and sell town bonds to fund this purchase and to levy taxes on property owned by white persons and poll taxes on white male citizens to pay off any debt incurred. The tax is limited to seventy-five cents per one hundred dollars of property value and three dollars per poll.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'white persons' and 'white male citizens' in the context of taxation for municipal purposes. While this indicates a racial distinction in the application of taxes, it does not appear to be a law designed to segregate or discriminate in the manner typically associated with Jim Crow laws (e.g., public accommodations, voting, marriage). The primary purpose of the act is to facilitate the acquisition of stock in a college by the town's trustees and to provide the financial mechanisms for doing so. The racial qualifier for taxation is a component of the financial mechanism, not the core discriminatory purpose of a Jim Crow law. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with an explicit racial indicator due to the direct mention of 'white persons' and 'white male citizens'.

classification.title: An Act to amend the charter of the town of Stanford, in Lincoln county, and to provide for the establishment and maintenance of a college or schools therein.
classification.summary: This act allows the board of trustees of Stanford, Kentucky, to levy taxes for the support of a college or schools. Crucially, it specifies that property taxes for municipal purposes and poll taxes for white male citizens over twenty-one years of age are subject to voter approval by qualified white voters. The tax collection is also explicitly limited to property belonging to white persons.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'qualified white voters' and 'property...belonging to white persons' in the context of levying taxes for educational institutions. This direct racial distinction in the application of taxes and the definition of who can vote on these matters is a clear indicator of Jim Crow-era legislation designed to maintain racial segregation and control. The category is 'education' because the taxes are for the purpose of supporting a college or schools. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1874.

classification.title: An Act to Amend an Act Entitled 'An Act to Incorporate Princeton College'
classification.summary: This act amends the incorporation of Princeton College. It specifies that after the town's board of trustees acquires ownership of the college's stock, trustees will be elected by qualified white voters of the town. Widows with children between six and twenty years old will also have voting rights, potentially by proxy. The board of trustees will consist of seven members, and the town's board of trustees will oversee the college's finances. The college's board will manage its affairs, appoint teachers, and set salaries.

This is a clear indicator of racial segregation in the electoral process for the college's governance, which is a characteristic of Jim Crow laws. The statute also mentions widows with children having voting rights, but this is a specific exception within a racially defined electorate. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' because it refers to 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and an 'act to which this is an amendment', suggesting it's part of legislative acts passed during a session. classification.summary: This act incorporates the Warren Presbyterian Church of Louisville, establishing it as a body-politic and corporate with perpetual succession and a common seal. It names the initial trustees and outlines their powers to fill vacancies.

The first section (pages 485-486) details rates for various construction elements like walls, dentals, chimneys, pilasters, and bay windows. This appears to be a statute related to construction or trade regulations. The second section (starting with CHAPTER 405) is an act to incorporate the Warren Presbyterian Church of Louisville. Neither of these sections contains any language or provisions that suggest they are Jim Crow laws. They do not mention race, nor do they establish any form of racial segregation or discrimination. It outlines how trustees are appointed, how vacancies are filled, their quorum for business, their ability to contract, issue bonds, purchase land for a house of worship, manage church property, make by-laws, sue and be sued, and report to the congregation. The act was approved on February 20, 1874.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act to incorporate the Mercer County Mechanical and Agricultural Association.
classification.summary: This act repeals a provision that extended the jurisdiction of the Harrodsburg police court over the grounds of the Mercer County Mechanical and Agricultural Association. It grants the association's board of directors the power to appoint special police to maintain order and arrest offenders on the association's grounds during fairs or exhibitions. Arrested individuals are to be taken before the presiding judge of the Mercer quarterly court or a justice of the peace.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the internal governance and policing of a private agricultural association. It does not mention race or create any racial distinctions in its provisions. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

CHAPTER 410 .
AN ACT for the benefitof James M. Bolton, jailerof Knox county.
WHEREAS ,It appears to the satisfactionof thisGeneral
Assembly that James J. Hart , of Knox county ,has been
found,by the verdictof a jury and judgment of a court,
on two occasions,to be a lunatic, and on the lastinquest
was found to be a pauper lunatic; and whereas ,it ap-
pears that said James J. Hart was in fact a pauper luna-
tic from the finding of the firstinquest ; and whereas , it
appears thatsaidlunaticcould not be receivedintoany
of the asylums of the State at that time for want of room ;
and whereas , from his condition it became necessary to
confine him and put him under a committee ,or in the cus-
tody of the jailerof said county ; and whereas ,itappears,
by the order of the Knox county court,that he was placed
in the custody of James M. Bolton , jailerof Knox coun-
ty, and that said Bolton had to keep him for one year;
and whereas ,thisGeneral Assembly has heretofore,by an
actapproved on the 28thday of March , 1872,made pro-
vision for the support and care of this class of unfortu-
nate people ; and whereas , in consequence of an over-
sightin the court holding the firstinquest,the question as
towhether he was a pauper or not was overlooked; there-
fore,Be itenactedbytheGeneralAssemblyoftheCommonwealthof
Kentucky :
§1 . That the Auditordraw hiswarrant upon theTrea-
sury of the State for the sum of two hundred dollarsin
favor of James M. Bolton, jailerof Knox county .
§2 . This act shalltake effectfrom and after its pas
sage .
Approved February 20, 1874.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of James M. Bolton, jailer of Knox county.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the Auditor to draw a warrant for $200 in favor of James M. Bolton, the jailer of Knox county. This payment is to compensate him for the care and custody of James J. Hart, a pauper lunatic, for one year. The statute notes that Hart was found to be a lunatic on two occasions, the last time as a pauper, and that state asylums lacked room for him at the time. An oversight in the initial inquest regarding his pauper status is also mentioned.
classification.reasoning: The statute is an act for the benefit of a specific individual, the jailer of Knox county, to compensate him for the care of a pauper lunatic. It details the circumstances surrounding the care of this individual, including the lack of space in state asylums and an oversight in a previous inquest. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to incorporate the town of Owingsville," approved March 10, 1870, and to amend an act entitled "An Act to amend an act to incorporate the town of Owingsville," approved February 17, 1874.
classification.summary: This statute details the administrative and judicial functions of the town of Owingsville, Kentucky. It outlines the salaries and duties of town officers, the handling of fines and forfeitures, penalties for officers failing to turn over property, and the establishment and jurisdiction of the Owingsville police court. The police court has exclusive jurisdiction over town ordinance violations and concurrent jurisdiction with the Bath quarterly court in criminal, penal, and civil cases. It also specifies procedures for civil actions, court terms, and fees for jailers related to town ordinance violations and prisoner confinement.
classification.reasoning: The statute focuses on the administrative and judicial organization of the town of Owingsville. It details the powers and procedures of the police court, including its jurisdiction over town ordinances and concurrent jurisdiction with other courts. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices targeting specific racial groups. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Owingsville
classification.summary: This act details the operational procedures of the Owingsville police court, including its status as a court of record, the finality of judgments based on fine amounts or controversy value, appeal processes, the style of process, the execution of process by the marshal, the destination of collected fines and forfeitures (town treasury), the right to replevy judgments, and the method of paying or working off fines by confinement in jail at a rate of two dollars per day. It also grants the council power to compel able-bodied male persons to work off fines.
classification.reasoning: The statute outlines the procedural aspects of a local police court in Owingsville, Kentucky. It covers aspects like jurisdiction, appeals, fines, and the enforcement of judgments. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The focus is purely on the legal and administrative functions of the court. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY. 1874.
classification.summary: This section of the 1874 Laws of Kentucky outlines various powers granted to towns, including the regulation of lotteries, the sale of spirituous liquors, licensing of various businesses and amusements, and the issuance of licenses by the town clerk. It specifies restrictions on selling liquor to minors and inebriates, and requires unanimous council votes for liquor licenses.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the regulatory powers of towns in Kentucky in 1874. It covers a range of topics such as lotteries, the sale of alcoholic beverages, licensing of various businesses (shopkeepers, taverns, druggists, etc.), and amusements. Crucially, there is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The regulations appear to be general in nature, applying to all residents and businesses within the town's jurisdiction. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 463 1874 .
classification.summary: This section of the Laws of Kentucky from 1874 outlines various powers granted to town councils. These include regulating liquor licenses, prohibiting sales on Sundays, holidays, election days, and court days, and the power to suspend licenses on any public day. It also grants councils the power to prevent and suppress riots, disorderly houses, markets, butchers, the storage of gunpowder, horse-racing, obstructing streets, immoral conduct, vagrants, prostitutes, and animals running at large. The statute does not contain any provisions that explicitly or implicitly discriminate based on race.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the powers of town councils in Kentucky in 1874. It covers a range of municipal regulations, including the licensing of liquor sales, public order, and animal control. After a thorough review of the text, there are no explicit mentions of race, nor are there any implicit discriminatory provisions that would suggest it is a Jim Crow law. The regulations appear to be general in nature and applicable to all residents of the town. Therefore, the statute is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a high confidence score. The category is 'other' as it does not fit into the specific categories provided. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the source information 'GenAssemb 1874.pdf' and the title 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 1874 .'.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Vanceburg
classification.summary: This act details the assessment of taxable property and the collection of taxes for street improvements in Vanceburg. It also establishes a poll tax for males between 16 and 50 years old, requiring them to either work on streets for two days or pay a fee. Those over 50 also pay a poll tax. Penalties are outlined for non-compliance. The tax revenue is specifically for grading and macadamizing streets.
classification.reasoning: The statute outlines municipal taxation and labor requirements for street improvements. It does not contain any explicit or implicit racial classifications or restrictions. The requirements apply to all male inhabitants within the specified age ranges, regardless of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

If a majority of the county court justices assent, a poll will be opened at the next regular election to vote on the proposition. If two-thirds of the qualified voters favor the subscription, the county judge will order the result to be recorded, and the county clerk will make the subscription on behalf of the county. The county court must then make provisions for payment using county bonds, with specific limitations on denomination, interest rate, and issuance conditions.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate The Chicago and South Atlantic Railroad Company of the State of Kentucky
classification.summary: This act incorporates "The Chicago and South Atlantic Railroad Company of the State of Kentucky" with a capital stock of $12,000,000. It outlines the proposed routes for the railroad, starting from the Ohio River and extending south through various Kentucky counties to the Tennessee line, with branches to Morristown and Knoxville, Tennessee. The purpose is to form a through line from Chicago to the South Atlantic coast. Commissioners are appointed to organize the company, open stock books, and receive subscriptions.
classification.reasoning: The statute clearly describes the incorporation of a railroad company and its proposed routes. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly in 1874.

Board styled sinking fund, and their duties.
§ 15. That on levying a tax, as provided for in this act, to pay the interest on bonds issued by the county court of any county, or of any city or town, it shall be the duty of the county court, city council, or town trustees making such levy, to appoint (3) three resident tax-payers of such county, city, or town, who shall be styled the board of commissioners of the sinking fund of such county, city, or town. The commissioners so appointed shall, before they proceed to discharge their duties, be sworn in the presence of the court, council, or trustees appointing them, faithfully to discharge their duties according to the best of their skill and judgment; they shall hold their office at the pleasure of the court, council, or trustees by whom they are appointed; they shall immediately appoint one of their own number treasurer, who shall execute bond, payable to the Commonwealth, with such security as shall be approved by the court, council, or trustees appointing the commissioners, conditioned for the faithful keeping and disbursing of all money coming to his hands as treasurer of such board; on which bond suit may be brought, from time to time, by and in the name of such county, city, or town, or by any other person injured by any breach of his bond, in any court having jurisdiction of the sum claimed in such suit. Such treasurer may be required, at any time, to give a new bond; and any surety in such bond shall be entitled to the same remedies for procuring additional or counter security as are now given to the sureties of guardians, administrators, &c. Said treasurer shall be allowed for his services such compensation, not exceeding (one) 1 per cent. on the money received and paid out by him, as may be allowed by the court, council, or trustees appointing such commissioners respectively. In case a vacancy shall occur, it shall be filled by the court, council, or trustees by whom such board was appointed.

The act also specifies the process for notifying the owner and confirming the report in county court.

classification.summary: This act incorporates Eginton Lodge, No. 490, of the Free and Accepted Masons in Whitley County, Kentucky. It grants the lodge corporate powers, including the ability to own property, contract, sue and be sued, and pass by-laws. It also specifies how legal notices should be served and allows the lodge to issue bonds.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute is an act to incorporate a specific Masonic lodge. It details the corporate powers and operational aspects of this lodge. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act incorporating the Sherburne Bridge Company."
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act from 1854 concerning the Sherburne Bridge Company. It modifies the voting power of shareholders, establishing a tiered system based on the number of shares owned. Shareholders with up to twenty shares get one vote per share. Those with over twenty and under thirty shares get one vote for every five shares over twenty. Shareholders with over thirty shares get one vote for every ten shares over thirty.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text is an amendment to an act incorporating the Sherburne Bridge Company. The amendment specifically addresses the voting power of shareholders based on the number of shares they own. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices related to race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the content is clearly about corporate governance and not racial discrimination.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to incorporate the World's Fair Association, of Woodford county," approved February 19, 1873.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act incorporating the World's Fair Association of Woodford County. It modifies the association's name, the location of the fair to be held near the borders of Woodford, Fayette, and Scott counties, and the number of directors from ten to forty. It also amends the types of beverages prohibited, changing from 'spirituous' to 'malt, fermented, or distilled'.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details amendments to an act incorporating a 'World's Fair Association'. The amendments concern the association's name, the location of the fair, the number of directors, and the types of beverages that are prohibited. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Catholic Cemetery of Louisville
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Catholic Cemetery of Louisville, granting it perpetual exemption from taxation and execution for debts. It outlines how corporate funds are to be applied, the management structure, and the process for purchasers of burial lots to receive deeds. The act specifies that lots are to be used exclusively for Catholic burials and will revert to the corporation if used for other purposes. It also mandates the corporation to keep records of sales and financial accounts.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to incorporate the town of Booneville, in Owsley county," approved March 1st, 1847.
classification.summary: This act amends the incorporation of the town of Booneville, Owsley County. It redefines the town's boundaries and establishes the election process and terms for a police judge and town marshal. It specifies that any person living within the town limits with the right to vote for members of the General Assembly can vote for these town officers. The police judge is to be elected every four years, and the marshal every two years. The act also outlines the process for filling vacancies and the qualifications for holding these offices (resident and qualified voter). The police judge must receive a commission from the Governor and take an oath similar to that of county judges.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the incorporation and governance of the town of Booneville, specifically defining its boundaries and the election of local officials like the police judge and town marshal. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The voting rights mentioned are tied to general eligibility for voting for the General Assembly, not a racial qualification. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Trustees of South Carrollton Male and Female Institute
classification.summary: This act establishes the Trustees of South Carrollton Male and Female Institute as a corporate body. It grants them perpetual existence, the power to use a corporate seal, sue and be sued, hold and dispose of property for the benefit of the high school, and ratify or make contracts. The board is also empowered to appoint a principal, professors, teachers, and instructors, and to establish by-laws and rules for the institution's governance. They can also regulate the course of studies and confer diplomas, honors, and degrees upon graduates.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled 'An Act to incorporate the Eminence College'
classification.summary: This act allows common school trustees in a district where Eminence College is located to subscribe to its capital stock. If they do, the county judge is authorized to levy a tax on the property of *white* tax-payers of the district to pay for this subscription. The college, in turn, must agree to teach common schools for *white* children in that district and receive public money for it. The act also specifies that the sense of *white* qualified voters must be taken before any subscription is made.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'white tax-payers' and 'white children' in relation to the funding and operation of schools. This indicates a clear racial distinction in the provision of educational services, which is a hallmark of Jim Crow legislation. The law mandates that public funds derived from white taxpayers will support a college that is then obligated to educate white children, implicitly excluding Black children from this specific arrangement. Therefore, it is classified as a Jim Crow law in the education category.

classification.summary: This act grants various powers to the governing bodies of incorporated towns in Kentucky. These powers include regulating meetings, appointing officers, maintaining public order and health, managing streets and public spaces, abating nuisances, preventing fires, and suppressing vice such as tippling houses, houses of ill fame, and gambling. It also allows for the regulation of livestock and the recovery of property.

classification.summary: This act prohibits the sale or gift of alcoholic beverages within a two-mile radius of E. J. Murphy's academy in Pembroke, Christian County, with exceptions for medicinal use. Violators face fines, with penalties split between the informer and the common school fund. Police judges and justices of the peace have concurrent jurisdiction with the circuit court.
classification.reasoning: The statute in question, Chapter 579 of the 1874 Kentucky Laws, is an act to prevent the sale or giving of alcoholic beverages within a two-mile radius of a specific academy. The text of the statute does not contain any mention of race, nor does it establish any differential treatment based on race. The restrictions and penalties apply to all individuals equally. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the absence of racial indicators is clear.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of the owners of Wabash Island.
classification.summary: This act makes it unlawful for individuals, other than landowners, to drive or transport cattle to Wabash Island. It also prohibits cattle from being at large on the island unless they belong to the landowners. If non-owner cattle are found, they can be impounded, and if not removed within three days after notice, they can be sold at public auction. The proceeds, after deducting impoundment fees, are to be paid to the county clerk for the owner's benefit. Owners removing their cattle must pay a daily fee per head.
classification.reasoning: The statute addresses the issue of livestock trespass on Wabash Island. It establishes regulations for the impoundment and sale of cattle found on the island that do not belong to the landowners. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The law is purely concerned with property rights and livestock management. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act for the relief of P. G. Smith and Alexander Hauchin
classification.summary: This act relieves P. G. Smith and Alexander Hauchin of Edmonson county from further responsibility as sureties for O. G. Moore, a former sheriff, regarding a balance of a judgment owed to the Commonwealth of Kentucky. It also stipulates that no further executions will be issued against them for this judgment, though their release will be considered a partial payment (pro tanto) and will not affect the liability of other sureties for their pro rata portion.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns a financial matter related to a sheriff's bond and the relief of sureties from their financial obligations. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The context is purely financial and legal relief for individuals involved in a debt to the Commonwealth. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to authorize the city of Owensboro to subscribe stock to the Vincennes, Petersburg, and Owensboro Railroad Company.
classification.summary: This act grants the city of Owensboro the authority to subscribe $100,000 to the stock of the Vincennes, Petersburg, and Owensboro Railroad Company. The subscription is contingent on the railroad company agreeing to complete the line to the Ohio River opposite Owensboro. The city can pay for the stock using its bonds, and the stock and any dividends will be pledged for the repayment of these bonds. The act also requires a voter referendum before the subscription can be made and outlines the process for levying taxes to pay for the bonds.
classification.reasoning: The statute authorizes a city to invest in a railroad company and outlines the financial mechanisms for doing so. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that would implicitly create racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act outlines the election process, qualifications, duties, and jurisdiction of police judges and marshals in specific towns within Kentucky. It details how contested elections are resolved, how vacancies are filled, and the oaths and bonds required for these officials. The police judge is granted concurrent jurisdiction with justices of the peace in civil and penal matters within the town, and concurrent jurisdiction with two justices of the peace in criminal matters within the county. The marshal is given the powers of a constable.

It specifies election procedures, qualifications, duties, and jurisdictional powers. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The focus is purely on the structure and operation of local law enforcement and judicial bodies. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Re-enact Chapter 83 of the General Statutes, Title 'Schools', So as to Provide for the Better Organization and Government of the Public Schools of the Town of Ashland.
classification.summary: This act establishes a board of education for the town of Ashland, Kentucky, granting them control over public schools, property, and funds. The board is empowered to make rules, appoint teachers, prescribe curriculum, and set admission qualifications. Section 11 states that all white children between six and twenty-one years of age within each district shall have equal admission rights to the schools of that district, with no tuition fees.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'white children' in Section 11, indicating a racial classification for educational access. While it grants equal rights to 'white children' within their districts, the explicit mention of race in the context of school admission strongly suggests a discriminatory framework, even if it doesn't explicitly mention other races being excluded. However, the prompt asks for Jim Crow laws, which typically involved segregation and disenfranchisement of Black people. This statute, by only mentioning 'white children' and not explicitly stating segregation or exclusion of other races, is not a direct Jim Crow law in the same vein as those mandating separate facilities or prohibiting intermarriage. It is a statute that *uses* race to define eligibility, which is a characteristic of the era, but not a direct segregation mandate. Given the explicit mention of race in defining eligibility, it is classified as 'yes' for Jim Crow, but with a high confidence that it's not a typical segregation law. The confidence is high because the racial indicator is explicit. The category is education. The document type is session laws as indicated by the source 'GenAssemb 1874.pdf'. Human review is not needed due to the explicit racial mention and clear category.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to provide for the establishment and regulation of public schools in the town of Ashland," approved March 12, 1872.
classification.summary: This act amends the establishment and regulation of public schools in Ashland. It grants the board of education the power to levy taxes for school purposes, including purchasing property, erecting buildings, and paying teachers. Notably, Section 16 explicitly states that only property belonging to white persons shall be taxed. Section 12 also mentions a poll-tax not to exceed one dollar on each white tithin in the district. Section 19 allows the admission of white children who are non-residents under certain conditions. Section 20 states the tax will not be levied until submitted to legally qualified white voters.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'white persons' in relation to taxation for school purposes (Section 16) and poll taxes (Section 12). It also specifies that the tax will be submitted to 'legally qualified white voters' (Section 20). These explicit racial distinctions in the funding and governance of public education are characteristic of Jim Crow era legislation, which often created separate and unequal systems based on race. Therefore, this statute is classified as Jim Crow with high confidence. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as an amendment to a previous act passed by the General Assembly in 1874.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to regulate the civil jurisdiction of the police court in the city of Hickman , Fulton county," approved February 9th, 1872.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act concerning the civil jurisdiction of the police court in Hickman, Fulton County. It expands the court's civil jurisdiction to cases where the matter in controversy does not exceed one hundred dollars (exclusive of interest and costs), provided the defendant resides within the city limits or is a non-resident of Kentucky. The jurisdiction is otherwise limited to the city's corporate limits. A key provision states that non-residents of the city are not compelled to serve as jurors in the city court.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the civil jurisdiction of a city court and jury service. While it includes a clause about not compelling non-residents to serve as jurors, this is a general jurisdictional and procedural matter and does not appear to have any explicit or implicit racial component. There is no mention of race, and the provisions do not suggest differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Big South Fork Lumber Company
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Big South Fork Lumber Company, granting it broad powers to conduct various businesses including lumber, manufacturing, mining, mercantile, and oil refining. It specifically authorizes the company to float and drive logs and lumber down the Big South Fork of the Cumberland River and its tributaries, erect and control booms and piers, enter upon private lands for these purposes (subject to damages), build roads, bridges, tramways, and railroads for transportation, and construct boats and rafts. The company is also empowered to build locks, dams, and canals to improve navigation and collect tolls for passage through locks.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the River Navigation Company
classification.summary: This act incorporates the River Navigation Company, granting it the authority to improve the Big South Fork of the Cumberland River by removing obstructions, building booms, locks, and dams. It outlines the tolls the company can charge for various types of watercraft and cargo, and establishes the process for electing company officers. The act specifies that the company must first remove existing obstructions at 'Big and Little Devil's Jumps' before collecting tolls.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the incorporation of a navigation company and the regulation of river traffic and tolls. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with full confidence, and the category is null.
